coredata.dc:title: Tribological properties of polyimide coating filled with solvent-free covalent carbon quantum dots nanofluids

coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Baokai
Pei, Xianqiang
Wang, Yanming
Wan, Shanhong
Zhao, Gai
Wang, Qihua
Wang, Tingmei
coredata.dc:description: Novel organic coatings were developed by incorporating solvent-free covalent carbon quantum dots nanofluids (CQDs NFs) into a polyimide (PI) matrix. The tribological properties of CQDs NFs/PI composite coatings were tested using a ball-on-disk tribometer, revealing that even a low loading of CQDs NFs significantly improved tribological performance of PI. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations and nanoindentation tests showed that CQDs NFs restricted PI molecular chain movement, enhancing mechanical properties. Additionally, CQDs NFs promoted the formation of stable transfer films on steel surfaces, improving wear resistance. This study confirms CQDs NFs' effectiveness in modifying PI composite coatings' friction and wear properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyimide
Carbon quantum dots
Tribological properties
MD simulation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparing process of PI composite coatings.

MD simulation model: (a) CQDs nanoparticles (b) CQDs NFs; (c), (d), and (e) are pure PI, CQDs/PI, and CQDs NFs/PI models respectively; (f) friction of PI through; friction of PI through (g) CQDs and (h) CQDs NFs.

Physicochemical properties of CQDs NFs: (a) TEM image of CQDs NFs, and the upper left inset is size distribution of CQDs NFs. (b) HRTEM image of CQDs NFs, and the upper right inset is FFT image of CQDs NFs. (c) FT-IR and (d) TGA curves of KH560-M2070, CQDs and CQDs NFs; (e) XPS survey spectra and (f) C 1 s spectra of CQDs NFs; Evolution of (g) modulus and (h) viscosity with increasing temperature; (i) complex viscosity-shear rate trace of CQDs NFs.

(a) TGA curves of different PI composite coatings; (b) Hardness and modulus as well as the corresponding (c) Load-Depth curves of different PI composite coatings during nanoindentation tests; (d) Mechanical properties, (e) Fractional free volume, and (f) Mean square displacement of CQDs NFs/PI composite models; (g-i) The Friactional free volume distribution in the PI composite models.

SEM images of fractured surfaces: (a) Pure PI and CQDs NFs/PI composite coatings with varying contents: (b) 0.5 wt%, (c) 1 wt%, (d) 3 wt%, (e) 5 wt%; (g) CQDs/PI; (h) CQDs/KH560-M2070/PI composite coatings. EDS elemental mapping of Si on fractured surfaces of (h) 3 wt% and (i) 5 wt% composite coatings.
Optical morphology of the scratched surface of different PI composite coatings after progressive scratch tests.
(a) COF curves and (b) COF and wear rate of PI composite coatings with different CQDs NFs content.

3D microscopic morphologies, longitudinal profiles and SEM worn surface of different PI composite coatings as well as corresponding counter balls: (a1, b1, c1, d1, e1) Pure PI, (a2, b2, c2, d2, e2) 0.5 wt%, (a3, b3, c3, d3, e3) 3 wt% and (a4, b4, c4, d4, e4) 5 wt%.
(a) COF curves and (b) COF and wear rate of PI composite coatings with different fillers.

3D microscopic morphologies, longitudinal profiles and SEM worn surface of different PI composite coatings as well as corresponding counter balls: (a1, b1, c1, d1, e1) CQDs/PI and (a2, b2, c2, d2, e2) CQDs/KH560-M2070/PI.
Fe 2p, C 1s, and O1s spectra of the steel ball surface lubricated with Pure PI (a-c) and 3 wt% CQDs NFs/PI composite coating (d-f).
The dynamic evolution of PI composite models under 50 MPa.
(a) The RDF and (b) Relative concentration of PI composite models with different fillers.
Diagram of the lubrication mechanism of CQDs NFs/PI composite coating.

head.ce:title: Tribological properties of polyimide coating filled with solvent-free covalent carbon quantum dots nanofluids

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 73000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Mechanics and Control for Aerospace Structures, Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Nanjing 210016, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Mechanics and Control for Aerospace Structures, Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Mechanics and Control for Aerospace Structures, Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Nanjing 210016, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel organic coatings were developed by incorporating solvent-free covalent carbon quantum dots nanofluids (CQDs NFs) into a polyimide (PI) matrix. The tribological properties of CQDs NFs/PI composite coatings were tested using a ball-on-disk tribometer, revealing that even a low loading of CQDs NFs significantly improved tribological performance of PI. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations and nanoindentation tests showed that CQDs NFs restricted PI molecular chain movement, enhancing mechanical properties. Additionally, CQDs NFs promoted the formation of stable transfer films on steel surfaces, improving wear resistance. This study confirms CQDs NFs' effectiveness in modifying PI composite coatings' friction and wear properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Friction and wear are significant issues that usually lead to energy waste, environmental pollution, and mechanical failure [1,2]. Therefore, effective lubrication solutions are essential. Solid lubricants are widely used in engineering and industrial sectors to improve the lifespan of equipment [3–6]. Polyimide (PI) composite coatings, for instance, are applied to large-tonnage equipment to reduce friction and wear [7,8].

However, pure PI has limited applications due to its insufficient wear resistance in these sectors [9]. Carbon-based nanomaterials, known for their chemical stability and environmental friendliness, are commonly blended into resins to enhance their properties. Even in small quantities, these nanomaterials can significantly improve the anti-wear characteristics of resins [10–12]. Discovered in 2004, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a type of emerging carbon-based nanomaterial with spherical or quasi-spherical structures less than 10 nm in diameter [13]. Unlike conventional carbon nanomaterials, CQDs have smaller, more uniform sizes, leading to better dispersion in dispersants. Studies have demonstrated the benefits of CQDs in reducing friction and wear [14,15]. Tang et al.[16] developed PEG molecule-functionalized carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQDs-PEG) using a hydrothermal method, achieving a 33.1 % reduction in the coefficient of friction (COF) and a 62.7 % decrease in wear volume compared to pure PEG200. Similarly, Hu et al.[4] synthesized CQDs as functional fillers for PI through a hydrothermal method, resulting in a reduction of the COF from 0.37 to 0.24 and a 63.0 % decrease in the wear rate.

Solvent-free fluids, introduced by Giannelis and colleagues [17,18], feature a core-shell structure with surface-modified core nanoparticles. These nanofluids' friction-reducing and anti-wear properties are attributed to the lubrication provided by the nanoparticles' core and the formation of a protective transfer films [19]. Numerous studies have confirmed the exceptional performance of these nanofluids as lubricants, additives, or polymer fillers [20–22]. Guo et al.[20] examined the tribological properties of epoxy resin (EP) enhanced with solvent-free ionic SiO2 nanofluids (SiO2 NFs), finding excellent tribological properties even in small amounts. Additionally, Cai et al.[23] prepared solvent-free ionic CQDs nanofluids, which reduced the COF by 73.5 % and decreased the wear volume by 82.8 % when used as PEG200 additives. Consequently, the combination of CQDs and KH560-M2070 promotes the chelation reaction between the organic chains and the tribo-pair while reducing friction and wear through the polishing effect of CQDs. This synergistic interaction provides robust protection within the PI matrix, resulting in outstanding anti-friction and anti-wear performance.

In this research, we synthesized CQDs NFs and evaluated their unique impact on the mechanical and tribological behavior of CQDs NFs/PI composite coatings. Notably, the study investigates the transfer films formed on the steel ball to elucidate the innovative lubrication mechanisms introduced by these composites. Furthermore, MD simulations were employed to explore the toughening mechanism of PI reinforced with CQDs NFs, providing a comprehensive understanding of their interactions. This integration of experimental findings and simulations highlights the distinct advantages of using CQDs NFs in enhancing polymer composite coatings, setting this work apart from previous studies on carbon-based nanofluids and PI composites. These insights pave the way for future advancements in the design and optimization of high-performance polymer composites.

Citric acid monohydrate (AR, 99.5 %) and ethyl acetate (AR, 99.5 %) were sourced from Shanghai Hushi, while γ-(2,3-epoxypropoxy) propytrimethoxysilane (KH560, AR, 98 %), 4,4′-(hexafluoroisopropylidene) diphthalic anhydride (6FDA, AR, 99 %), and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, AR, 99 %) were supplied by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. provided 4,4′-diaminodiphenyl ether (ODA, AR, 98 %). Jeffamine M2070 was obtained from Huntsman Corporation, and methanol was procured from China National Pharmaceutical Group Corporation. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. Ultra-pure water was produced using a laboratory water purification system (Spring-R10, Research Water Purification Technology Co., Ltd.).

CQDs were synthesized via direct pyrolysis using citric acid monohydrate as the precursor [24]. In brief, 12 g of citric acid monohydrate was heated in a 200 ℃ oil bath until it transformed into a viscous red-black liquid. Ultra-pure water was then added, and the mixture was sonicated until dissolved. Next, 100 mL of ethyl acetate was added, shaken, and the upper layer of the clear amber solution was separated and placed in a spinning bottle. An equal amount of ultra-pure water was added, and the mixture was evaporated. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried to obtain CQDs.

For the preparation of CQDs NFs, CQDs were grafted with covalent linkers KH560 and M2070. Equimolar amounts of KH560 and M2070 were added to 100 mL of a 10 wt% methanol mixture and stirred for 24 h at 50 ℃. Then, 2.4 g of CQDs were added and stirred for 16 h at 35 ℃. The mixture was evaporated using rotary evaporation, placed in a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 3500), and dialyzed. Finally, the dialyzed liquid was transferred to a vacuum oven to dry completely.
Figs. 1–2.

The PI composite coating was prepared following a previous report [8]. The PI composite coating is denoted as x wt%, where x represents the weight percentage of CQDs NFs in the PI matrix. For 3 wt%, CQDs NFs (0.1993 g) were added to a solution of ODA (2.0024 g) in NMP (47.0 mL) in a 250 mL three-neck flask. First, vacuum bake at 50 ℃, 90 ℃, and 200 ℃ for 120 min to remove residual air and solvent. Then, vacuum bake the polyamine solution at 300 ℃ for 1 h to complete the imidization process. Additionally, to explore the synergistic effect of the CQDs core and the crown structure (KH560-M2070), PI composite coatings with the same CQDs core mass fraction were prepared and named CQDs/PI and CQDs/KH560-M2070/PI. Please refer to the supplementary file for the completion of imidation of all samples (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: MD simulation of PI composite coating
ce:section.ce:para: The details of MD simulation can be seen in the Supplementary file.

The morphology and microstructure of CQDs NFs were observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200S, Netherlands). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bruker Tensor 27, Germany), Ultraviolet and visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometry (TU-1810DSPC, Persee, China) were utilized to analyze surface functional groups. The thermal stability of the nanofluid was assessed with a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, DSC-TG STA 449F3, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA), using a monochromatic Al Kα 1486.6 eV light source, 15 kV voltage, 10 mA beam, and CAE analyzer scanning mode, was employed to determine the chemical composition of CQDs NFs. The binding energy of all samples was calibrated to the C1s peak at 284.8 eV, with background subtraction using the Smart function and Gaussian-Lorentzian peak models applied to high-resolution spectra. The modulus and hardness of PI composite coatings were measured using a nanoindentation instrument (STeP E400, Anton Paar GmbH, Austria). A rheometer (Anton Paar, MCR-302, Austria) with a plate-plate system was used to investigate the liquid behaviors and complex viscosities of the CQDs NFs. A scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-7610F, Japan) equipped with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) characterized the worn surfaces of various PI composite coatings and the microstructure of the corresponding counter balls.

The PI composite coating were tested on an MFT-5000 tribometer using the ball-on-disk mode. A 5 mm diameter bearing steel ball (GCr15, HRC 60 ± 2) was used as the friction pair. The experiments were conducted at a sliding velocity of 0.1 m/s, a load of 2 N, and a friction radius of 10 mm at room temperature.

TEM characterization ( Fig. 3a) revealed that the core nanoparticles of CQDs NFs have quasi-spherical structures with an average size of approximately 1.9 nm. HRTEM images (Fig. 3b) showed lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (110) crystal plane of graphene [25]. UV-Vis analysis showed that CQDs NFs had absorption peaks at 260 nm and 330 nm, attributed to CC bonds and CO bonds, while CA, being colorless and transparent, showed minimal absorption below 230 nm [26]. These results confirmed the successful preparation of CQDs. (Fig. S3). The elemental compositions and chemical states of the samples were analyzed using FT-IR and XPS spectra. The synthesis process of CQDs NFs was tracked using FT-IR (Fig. 3c), which displayed asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of -NH2 in M2070 at 3365 cm−1 and 3296 cm−1, respectively [27]. Peaks at 850 cm−1 and 1110 cm−1, associated with O-Si-O and Si-O-Si, indicated the covalent bonding of KH560-M2070 to the CQDs surface via dehydration condensation, with KH560 acting as a coupling agent [28]. XPS analysis (Fig. 3d) confirmed the presence of Si elements from KH560-M2070 [29]. The C 1 s XPS fine spectrum (Fig. 3e) of CQDs NFs showed peaks at 284.8, 286.4, and 288.9 eV, corresponding to C-C/CC, -COOR, and C-O/C-N, respectively [30]. TGA analysis (Fig. 3f) indicated no weight loss below 350 ℃, demonstrating the high thermal stability of CQDs NFs. The TGA also quantified the KH560-M2070 shell content on the CQDs, revealing a mass ratio of approximately 27.8 wt% for the CQDs core and 72.2 wt% for the KH560-M2070 shell [31]. The loss modulus of CQDs NFs was higher than the storage modulus across the measured temperature range, indicating a dissipation-dominated liquid-like behavior (Fig. 3g) [32]. The viscosity of CQDs NFs decreased with increasing temperature due to the greater flexibility of KH560-M2070 at higher temperatures (Fig. 3h) [27], exhibiting Newtonian behavior with constant viscosity over the range of shear rates studied (Fig. 3i) [22].

The decomposition temperature of pure PI was 543 ℃, and when 3 wt% of CQDs NFs was added, the thermal decomposition temperature slightly reduced to 541 ℃ ( Fig. 4a, S2), indicating that the CQDs NFs did not significantly impact the thermal properties of the PI matrix. The main factor in the reduced thermal stability of the composite coatings is the decomposition of the organic chain KH560-M2070. The nanomechanical behaviors of PI and its nanocomposites were examined using nanoindentation. The micro-mechanical properties and Load-Depth curves of the PI composite coatings are shown (Fig. 4b and c). Pure PI exhibited a modulus of 4.83 GPa and hardness of 429 MPa (Fig. 4b). With 3 wt% CQDs NFs, these values increased to 5.2 GPa and 470 MPa. However, higher concentrations of CQDs NFs resulted in diminished micro-mechanical properties. The Load-Depth curves (Fig. 4c) confirmed the enhanced resilience of the CQDs NFs/PI composite coating at 3 wt% CQDs NFs, showing lower indentation depth and higher recovery volume compared to the pure PI matrix. Excessive CQDs NFs content deteriorated the composite's properties, likely due to the presence of too many flexible organic molecular chains in the PI matrix. The content of CQDs significantly influenced the micro-mechanical properties of PI. At 5 wt% CQDs NFs, the properties notably decreased, and sample error increased, likely due to poor dispersion at high CQDs content. The comparison between 3 wt% CQDs/PI and CQDs/KH560-M2070 showed that KH560-M2070 modification of the CQDs core surface improved the micro-mechanical properties of the PI composite coating; without it, the properties declined [33,34]. MD simulations provided further insight into the enhancement mechanisms of PI composite coatings (Fig. 4d). Similar to nanoindentation results, the Young's modulus and shear modulus of PI composite models showed consistent patterns, calculated under the COMPASS force field. The increase in fractional free volume (FFV) in PI composite models might be due to increased cross-linking between molecular chains (Fig. 4e) [35,36], leading to a more stable network structure (Fig. 4g-i) that enhances mechanical strength and plasticity. These fillers increase free volume by forming microscopic voids or holes while also improving mechanical properties. Additionally, the mean square displacement (MSD) indicated that a more stable network structure restricted the movement of PI molecules (Fig. 4f), thereby increasing mechanical properties [37].

The fracture mechanisms of pure PI and PI composite coatings were analyzed using SEM ( Fig. 5). The images reveal that the fracture surface of pure PI exhibits distinct layered and wavy patterns (Fig. 5a), indicating brittle failure with limited crack propagation resistance (arrows in Fig. 5a) [38]. In contrast, with the introduction of CQDs NFs (Fig. 5b-d), the wavy patterns gradually diminish, resulting in a smoother surface (arrows in Fig. 5d). This enhancement likely stems from the synergistic effect of CQDs and the organic chain KH560-M2070, causing a transition from brittle to ductile fracture [39]. The fracture surface at 3 wt% CQDs (Fig. 5d) is relatively smooth, indicating restricted molecular movement (Fig. 4f), tighter molecular packing, and enhanced mechanical properties.

At 5 wt% CQDs NFs (Fig. 5e), while the CQDs NFs are well-dispersed (Fig. 5i), disordered grooves appear on the surface, likely due to an excessive amount of CQDs that disrupts the PI matrix and negatively impacts the material. In contrast, at approximately 0.8 wt% CQDs (Fig. 5f), the fracture surface of the CQDs/PI composite coating remains smooth, displaying only minor wavy textures (arrows in Fig. 5f). However, similar disordered grooves (arrows in Fig. 5g) are noted on the fracture surface of the CQDs/KH560-M2070/PI composite coating (Fig. 5g), indicating that the excessive and disordered KH560-M2070 organic chains are incompatible with the PI molecular structure, leading to structural disorganization and reduced overall performance of the composite coating.

Scratch resistance tests ( Fig. 6) were conducted on the PI composite coatings to evaluate their adhesion strength, a crucial factor influencing their tribological performance and durability [40]. For PI composite coatings, spalling along the scratch track initiated at the critical load (Fig. 6). Initial stage, the surface is primarily influenced by the plowing mechanism, exhibiting few or no cracks. As the pressure increases, periodic and symmetrical V-shaped cracks emerge, extending outward, alongside random localized peeling. This is followed by periodic symmetric spalling, characterized by straight-line stretching, leading to partial stratification of the coating. Ultimately, continuous and severe delamination of the PI coating occurs, resulting in large-scale spallation on both sides of the scratch, exposing the substrate. This failure resulted from the synergistic effects of tensile and shear stresses, leading to separation between the pure PI coating and the substrate under high normal load. In contrast, composite coatings with added CQDs, CQDs NFs, and CQDs/KH560-M2070 showed improved adhesion, enhancing overall performance. As the CQDs NFs content increased from 0.5 wt% to 3 wt%, the critical load for failure rose, peaking at 39 N (Fig. 6b-d). However, at 5 wt% CQDs NFs, the critical load dropped below that of coatings with CQDs and CQDs/KH560-M2070 alone (Fig. 6f, g).

Interestingly, the CQDs/KH560-M2070 composite coating did not show a significant improvement in adhesion compared to the CQDs/PI composite coating, suggesting that the mechanical doping of KH560-M2070 had no substantial effect on the adhesion properties of PI composite coatings. Overall, The PI composite coatings exhibited excellent adhesion properties, demonstrating the existence of the synergistic effect between CQDs and KH560-M2070.

The fiber content had a significant influence on the tribological properties of the PI composite coatings. CQDs NFs/PI composite coatings with different concentrations (0.5 to 5 wt%) were evaluated, revealing notable trends in both the coefficient of friction (COF) and wear rate. As seen in Fig. 7a, the COF of pure PI sharply increased to 0.34 during the running-in stage before stabilizing at around 0.30. As friction continued, the wear depth of pure PI increased, causing the COF to rise to approximately 0.42. However, when CQDs NFs were added to the PI matrix at concentrations between 0.5 and 5 wt%, the COF decreased significantly, exhibiting a U-shaped trend: it dropped to 0.32, 0.30, 0.26, and 0.29. This trend indicates that insufficient fiber content led to a higher and unstable COF, while excessive organic chain content destabilizes the system, also resulting in an increased COF. The wear rate followed a similar U-shaped trend. At a 5 wt% CQDs NFs concentration, the wear rate was 0.83 × 10−5 mm3/Nm, which is 74 % of the wear rate of the pure PI matrix (3.14 ×10−5 mm3/Nm) but still higher than the wear rate at 3 wt% CQDs NFs. Even at a low concentration of 0.5 wt%, the inclusion of CQDs NFs significantly reduced the wear rate by about 38 %, bringing it down to 1.96 × 10−5 mm3/Nm.

This study demonstrates that the optimal concentration of CQDs NFs is crucial for achieving the best tribological performance in PI composite coatings. The balance between COF reduction and wear rate minimization occurs around 3 wt% CQDs NFs, where the material exhibits enhanced lubrication and wear resistance properties without compromising system stability.

Fig. 8 illustrates the morphology of CQDs NFs/PI composite coatings and the corresponding transfer films formed on the steel balls after tribological tests. The worn surface of pure PI shows a wide and deep furrow, indicative of severe abrasive wear (Fig. 8a1, b1). The SEM images further confirm this with large adhesion pits and deep furrows, highlighting the material's vulnerability to wear without any reinforcement. When CQDs NFs were incorporated into the PI matrix, the wear depth and width decreased significantly compared to pure PI. As the content of CQDs NFs increased, the wear scar became shallower and smoother (Fig. 8b1-b4). The best tribological properties were observed at 3 wt% CQDs NFs, where the composite coating facilitated the formation of continuous and compact carbon-based transfer films (Fig. S5), which effectively protected the surface during sliding and reduced wear. Insufficient or excessive CQDs NFs content led to the formation of incomplete protective films, resulting in wider and deeper furrows on the worn surface. At 5 wt% CQDs NFs, the tribological properties of the composite deteriorated (Fig. 8a4-d4). The excessive concentration reduced the toughness of the composite, leading to abrasive wear and the generation of particulate debris. This debris disrupted the formation of effective transfer films and abraded the surface of the counter ball, as seen in the SEM images (Fig. 8e4). These findings emphasize the importance of optimizing the concentration of CQDs NFs in PI composite coatings. The 3 wt% CQDs NFs content strikes the best balance, enhancing the tribological properties through the synergistic effect between CQDs and the KH560-M2070 modification, while too much or too little of the additive compromises the material's properties.

To explore the synergy between CQDs and KH560-M2070, tribological tests were performed on coatings with CQDs, CQDs/KH560-M2070, and CQDs, as shown in Fig. 9. The COF curves (Fig. 9a) showed significantly reduced fluctuations and were relatively smooth after adding moderate amounts of CQDs, CQDs/KH560-M2070, and CQDs NFs. The average COF decreased to 0.26, 0.30, and 0.27, respectively, indicating that CQDs are effective fillers in PI due to their small, uniform particle size and chemically inert surface. Incorporating 0.8 wt% CQDs alone reduced the wear rate from 3.14 × 10−5 mm³ /Nm to 2.16 × 10−5 mm³ /Nm, while the friction coefficient dropped from 0.42 to 0.30 (Fig. 9b). Adding CQDs with KH560-M2070 only improved friction-reduction without a significant synergistic effect. However, incorporating CQDs NFs into the PI matrix greatly enhanced friction-reduction and anti-wear properties. The 3 wt% sample (0.8 wt% CQDs core) reduced the friction coefficient and wear rate by 37 % and 88 %, respectively, compared to pure PI.

This substantial improvement is attributed to the synergistic effect of CQDs and KH560-M2070, demonstrating that even a low loading of CQDs can greatly enhance the tribological properties of the composite coating.

Fig. 10 illustrates the surface morphology of PI composite coatings filled with CQDs, CQDs/KH560-M2070, and the corresponding counter balls after sliding tests. The incorporation of 0.8 wt% CQDs into the PI matrix significantly reduced the ploughing grooves on the worn surface (Fig. 10a1-d1). These ploughing grooves were induced by the abrasive dust generated during the friction process, which adversely affected the material's anti-wear properties. This reduction was associated with the mitigation of abrasive wear thanks to enhanced mechanical properties of investigated composite coating. Interestingly, the simultaneous incorporation of KH560-M2070 did not improve the mechanical properties of the composites but helped further reduce the ploughing grooves on the surface (Fig. 10a2-d2). This contributed to the decreased friction coefficient and wear rate. Even so, materials transferred to the counter ball surface did not form compact transfer films when sliding against coatings filled with CQDs and CQDs/KH560-M2070 (Fig. 10e1, e2). In this regard, the coating's wear resistance coatings was not significantly influenced by these transferred materials.

To elucidate the chemical structures of transfer films, XPS analyses were conducted on the counter ball surfaces slid against pure PI and CQDs NFs/PI composite coating. In the C 1 s fine spectrum ( Fig. 11b, e), the peak at 284.8 eV remained consistent for both pure PI and CQDs NFs/PI composite coating, serving as the calibration reference for the XPS analysis. Even slid against pure PI, the counter ball surface was partly oxidized to Fe2O3 and FeO as is evidenced from the peaks at 710.9 and 724.3 eV in its Fe2p XPS spectra, as well as the peaks at 530.4 and 531.6 eV in its O1s XPS spectra [41] (Fig. 11a, c). In contrast, the Fe 2p XPS spectra of the ball surface slid against CQDs NFs/PI composite coatings exhibited notable differences (Fig. 11d), in which the peak at 707.2 eV indicated the formation of iron-organic compounds (Fe(CO)x) through chelation reactions [32]. This helped improve the bonding of transfer films to the counter ball surfaces and was beneficial for the modification of the composite coating's tribological properties.

Fig. 12 illustrates the dynamic behavior of modeled PI composites during shearing under 50 MPa, from which the failure of PI molecules induced by shear was revealed. For pure PI, the deformation reached 204.2 Å, which was significantly higher than those of CQDs/PI (152.8 Å) and CQDs NFs/PI (142.9 Å). This was understandable considering the fact that pure PI was characterized with low fractional free volume (FFV) and absence of micro-voids. This characteristic made its network instable under heavy load and was responsible for its greater deformation compared to CQDs/PI and CQDs NFs/PI composites. In contrast, the inclusion of CQDs NFs introduced a supportive network structure within the PI matrix, which effectively restricted the movement of the molecular chains and thereby reduced deformation under shear stress [42].

The Radial distribution function (RDF) quantifies the likelihood of locating a pair of particles at a specific distance from each other, offering insights into the microscopic structure and spatial arrangement of a system. In this study, we analyzed the RDF values ( Fig. 13a) between Fe and the PI composite to assess interaction strength during the friction process. [43,44]. The results reveal that CQDs NFs/PI exhibit the lowest RDF values, indicating weaker interactions, while CQDs/PI show the highest values, reflecting stronger interactions, with pure PI positioned between the two. This suggests that during the friction process, increased van der Waals forces and other interactions lead to more PI molecular chains interacting with iron atoms. Notably, the RDF value for pure PI is higher than that for the CQDs NFs composite, signifying its stronger interaction with Fe atoms. A comparison of the RDF values for CQDs NFs and CQDs indicates that CQDs NFs possess a greater ability to reduce the interaction between PI molecular chains and iron atoms, thanks to the synergistic effect of CQDs with KH560-M2070. This demonstrates that the friction-reducing capability of CQDs NFs surpasses that of CQDs. Additionally, when the variation in relative atomic concentration along the (0 0 1) direction is considered (Fig. 13b), pure PI had a larger peak area of atomic concentration in contact with Fe (Table S1) compared to the CQDs/PI and CQDs NFs/PI composites. This was associated with the fact that the presence of CQDs NFs and their network structure restricted the movement of PI molecular chains, which decreased the atomic concentration at the interface between PI composites and the counter surface [45].

Based on SEM, XPS and MD simulation results, the wear mechanism of the CQDs NFs/PI composite coating is illustrated in Fig. 14. Initially, CQDs NFs were gradually released from PI matrix. The fluidity of the KH560-M2070 shell facilitated the even distribution and anchoring of CQDs NFs on the matrix surface, leading to the rapid formation of transfer films. During the sliding process, the CQDs core helped repair the wear damages to the transfer films, thus enhancing their anti-wear performance. Therefore, the exceptional tribological properties of the CQDs NFs filled composite coatings were attributed to the synergistic effects between the CQDs core and KH560-M2070, among which the CQDs core facilitated the formation of robust transfer films with good load-bearing capacity and the flexible KH560-M2070 promoted the spreading and self-healing of the transfer films. MD simulations and nanoindentation tests confirmed that CQDs NFs restricted the movement of PI molecular chains (Fig. 14a, c), thereby improving the coatings' mechanical properties (Fig. 14b). Both of the above factors contributed to the excellent tribological properties of studied composite coatings in the present study.

In this study, solvent-free covalent CQDs NFs were synthesized and uniformly integrated into PI matrix to develop wear-resistant and lubricating coatings. The coatings' tribological properties were extensively investigated by both experiments and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. The main conclusions are as follows:

(1) Even a low concentration (0.5 wt%) of CQDs NFs resulted in significant reduction in the friction and wear of PI coating highlighting the superior effectiveness of CQDs NFs in modifying the tribological properties of PI composites.

(2) The formation of stable transfer films governed the composite coatings' wear resistance, which was enhanced with the incorporation of CQDs NFs thanks to the tribo-chemical reactions between the CQDs NFs/PI composite and the counter steel ball.

(3) CQDs NFs, on the one hand, effectively restricted the movement of PI molecular chains leading to improved mechanical properties, and, on the other hand, reduced the degree of deformation of the composite under tribological loadings, thereby protecting the PI matrix during sliding friction.
The proposed contents are original and not involving plagiarism with copyright infringement issues. I hereby declare and guarantee those are true, I claimed.

Tingmei Wang: Supervision, Conceptualization. Qihua Wang: Supervision, Conceptualization. Gai Zhao: Methodology, Conceptualization. Shanhong Wan: Methodology, Conceptualization. Yanming Wang: Methodology, Conceptualization. Xian-Qiang Pei: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Baokai Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.
